walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up the classic trivia showdown! Place the circular game board in the center of the table. You will see a wheel-and-spoke design with an outer ring of colored spaces, six spokes connecting to a center hub, and six category headquarters where the spokes meet the outer ring. Make sure everyone can see and reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Learn the Six Categories
walkthrough.text: Each color on the board represents a trivia category. Blue is Geography. Pink is Entertainment. Yellow is History. Brown is Art and Literature. Green is Science and Nature. Orange is Sports and Leisure. Take a moment to point out where each color's headquarters space is on the outer ring. These are the spaces where you earn your pie wedges.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Question Cards
walkthrough.text: Place the question cards in the card holder with the questions facing the reader. Each card has six questions, one per category color. When you land on a space, another player reads you the question matching that space's color. Make sure the card box is within reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Playing Piece
walkthrough.text: Each player selects one of the pie-shaped playing pieces. These circular tokens have six empty wedge slots around the edge. As you earn wedges by answering correctly at category headquarters, you will fill in each slot with the matching colored wedge. Start with all six slots empty.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Scoring Wedges
walkthrough.text: Give each player one scoring wedge in each of the six category colors. That is six wedges per player total. Keep them nearby but not in your playing piece yet. You will earn and insert them throughout the game. These wedges are your progress tracker toward victory.

walkthrough.title: Place Everyone on the Center Hub
walkthrough.text: All players place their playing piece on the center hub space to begin. The center hub is the starting point and also the finishing point. You will need to return here at the end of the game to answer the final winning question. Roll the die to determine who goes first, with the highest roll earning the starting position. Play proceeds clockwise.

bullets: Place circular board in the center — wheel-and-spoke design

summary.title: Place Everyone on the Center Hub

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Trivial Pursuit is a race to prove your trivia knowledge across six categories. Your goal is to earn one pie wedge from each category headquarters, then return to the center hub and answer one final question to win. The first player to complete all six wedges and nail the final question wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Roll and Move
walkthrough.text: On your turn, roll the die and move your playing piece that exact number of spaces in any direction you choose. You can move along the outer ring clockwise or counterclockwise, or move along any spoke toward or away from the center hub. You must use your full roll and cannot stop short.

walkthrough.title: Answer a Question
walkthrough.text: When you land on a space, another player draws a question card and reads you the question matching the color of your space. Give your answer clearly. Once you state your answer, it is final and cannot be changed. There is no official time limit, but many groups use a house rule of thirty seconds to one minute.

walkthrough.title: Correct Answer: Keep Going
walkthrough.text: If you answer correctly, roll again and take another turn immediately. There is no limit to how many consecutive correct answers you can chain together. A hot streak can carry you across the board and earn multiple wedges in a single turn. Momentum is everything in Trivial Pursuit.

walkthrough.title: Wrong Answer: Turn Ends
walkthrough.text: If you answer incorrectly, your turn ends immediately and play passes to the next player clockwise. The question card goes to the back of the card box regardless of whether you answered correctly or not. No peeking at upcoming questions!

walkthrough.title: Earning Pie Wedges
walkthrough.text: You can only earn a pie wedge by landing on one of the six category headquarters spaces on the outer ring and answering that category's question correctly. When you do, insert the matching colored wedge into your playing piece. You can only earn one wedge per category. If you land on a headquarters for a wedge you already have, a correct answer simply lets you roll again.

walkthrough.title: Movement Flexibility
walkthrough.text: You have complete freedom in choosing your direction each turn. You can move clockwise or counterclockwise on the outer ring. You can enter a spoke from the outer ring to head toward the center, or exit the center hub along any spoke. You can even transition between the outer ring and a spoke during a single move as long as the paths connect.

walkthrough.title: Roll Again Spaces
walkthrough.text: Some editions of the board include Roll Again spaces on the outer ring. If you land on one of these, you immediately roll again without answering a question. This gives you free extra movement. Not all editions have these spaces, so check your specific board.

walkthrough.title: The Final Challenge
walkthrough.text: Once you have collected all six wedges, head back to the center hub. You must land on the hub by exact die roll. When you reach it, your opponents choose which category your final question will come from. They will almost certainly pick your weakest category. Answer correctly and you win the game immediately!

walkthrough.title: Failed Final Question
walkthrough.text: If you reach the center hub and answer the final question incorrectly, your turn ends. On your next turn, you must leave the hub and come back again on a future turn. You will face a new final question each time you return. The opponents get to choose the category again, so prepare for another round of your weakest subject.

walkthrough.title: Team Play Variant
walkthrough.text: Trivial Pursuit works well with teams. Players form teams and share a single playing piece. The team discusses before giving one answer. Team play is especially fun with larger groups because it lets more people participate in each question. Pair players with complementary knowledge strengths for the best team balance.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Trivia Night
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Trivial Pursuit! We are going to walk through the opening turns of a four-player game. Our players are Maya, Jake, Tina, and Leo. Each one has a different knowledge profile, which makes the game interesting. Follow along even if your group has a different number of players.

walkthrough.title: Starting from the Hub
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts on the center hub. Maya rolls first and gets a five. She wants to head toward the Pink headquarters for Entertainment because that is her strongest category. From the center hub, she picks a spoke and counts five spaces. She lands on a green space on the outer ring, not quite at the Pink headquarters yet. An opponent reads her a Science and Nature question.

walkthrough.title: Maya Answers Correctly
walkthrough.text: The question is about which planet has the most moons. Maya answers Jupiter. That is correct! She gets to roll again immediately. She rolls a three and moves along the outer ring, landing right on the Pink headquarters. Another Entertainment question comes her way. She knows this one too and earns her first pink wedge. Two correct answers in a row kept her momentum going.

walkthrough.title: Jake's Turn: Planning a Route
walkthrough.text: Jake rolls a four and heads toward the Blue headquarters for Geography. He knows geography well and wants to get his strongest category out of the way first. He lands on a yellow space and gets a History question. He answers incorrectly and his turn ends. No worries, he is positioned close to the Blue headquarters for his next turn.

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Random Movement
walkthrough.text: Tina rolls a six and moves counterclockwise along the outer ring without really thinking about where she is heading. She lands on an orange space, answers a Sports and Leisure question incorrectly, and her turn ends. Tina moved randomly instead of aiming for a specific headquarters. She is now far from any headquarters she needs.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Aim for a Headquarters
walkthrough.text: Every move should bring you closer to a category headquarters you still need. Look at the board, count the spaces, and choose the direction that puts you on or near your target. Moving without a destination wastes turns and lets other players pull ahead. Even if you cannot reach a headquarters this turn, position yourself so a reasonable roll next turn gets you there.

walkthrough.title: Leo's Turn: Using Spokes as Shortcuts
walkthrough.text: Leo rolls a three and heads down a spoke toward the center hub. Why? Because the spoke connects to the outer ring near the Brown headquarters for Art and Literature, which Leo needs. Next turn he can exit the hub on a different spoke and reach it quickly. Spokes are shortcuts that let you cross the board much faster than circling the outer ring.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Building Momentum
walkthrough.text: Maya continues her hot streak. She rolls, lands on the Yellow headquarters, and nails a History question about the French Revolution. That is two wedges in the bank already. The key to Maya's success is that she is routing through headquarters spaces efficiently, picking up wedges as she goes rather than zigzagging randomly.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two: Avoiding Weak Categories
walkthrough.text: Jake keeps circling the outer ring to avoid the Brown headquarters for Art and Literature. He knows it is his weakest category and dreads getting a question wrong there. But after four turns of avoidance, he has only two wedges while Maya has four. Jake forgot that you must eventually face all six categories. Avoiding your weakest one just delays the inevitable.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Face Weak Categories Early
walkthrough.text: Go for your weakest category headquarters early in the game. If you fail the question, you have plenty of turns to come back and try again. If you get lucky and answer correctly, you have eliminated your biggest obstacle. Saving your worst category for last means you might be stuck circling back multiple times at the end of the game while opponents cruise to victory.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three: Heading to Hub Without All Wedges
walkthrough.text: Tina has four wedges and rolls a number that would land her on the center hub. Excited, she moves there, forgetting that she still needs Green and Orange wedges. She cannot attempt the final question without all six. Now she has to leave the hub next turn and go get those remaining wedges. She wasted a turn by not checking her playing piece first.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Your Wedges Before Heading to the Hub
walkthrough.text: Before moving toward the center hub, always check your playing piece. Count your wedges. You need all six before the final question attempt counts. Moving to the hub early accomplishes nothing except wasting a turn. This is especially important in the late game when excitement can override careful planning.

walkthrough.title: The Race to Six Wedges
walkthrough.text: Maya collects her fifth wedge and plans her route to the final headquarters. Jake finally tackles Art and Literature and gets lucky with a question about Shakespeare. Tina backtracks to pick up her missing wedges. Leo has been quietly and efficiently collecting wedges through smart spoke usage. The game is getting tense as multiple players approach six wedges.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have just experienced the key moments and common pitfalls of Trivial Pursuit. You learned to aim for headquarters spaces deliberately, face weak categories early rather than avoiding them, and always check your wedges before heading to the center hub. The combination of knowledge and smart board movement is what separates winners from the pack.

walkthrough.title: Roll the Die
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on movement planning and category prioritization.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to categories, board layout, and special spaces.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Time to prove what you know!

summary.title: Roll the Die
bullets: Check out General Tips → movement planning and category prioritization
Reference the Appendix → categories, board layout, special spaces
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Route to Headquarters
walkthrough.text: Before every roll, look at the board and identify which category headquarters you want to reach. Count the spaces and figure out which direction gives you the best chance of landing on it. Smart routing means passing through or near multiple headquarters in sequence rather than zigzagging randomly. Think two or three moves ahead and plan a circuit that hits your remaining headquarters efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Use Spokes to Cross the Board
walkthrough.text: The spokes connecting the outer ring to the center hub are your express lanes. Instead of circling half the outer ring to reach a distant headquarters, cut through the center hub via a spoke. From the hub, you can exit on any spoke and reach the opposite side of the board in just a few moves. Players who use spokes effectively reach headquarters much faster than those who stay on the outer ring.

walkthrough.title: Build Momentum Through Strong Categories
walkthrough.text: Route your path so that you pass through spaces of your strongest categories on the way to headquarters. Each correct answer gives you another roll. Stringing together correct answers in your strong categories gives you extra moves to reach the headquarters you need. Think of strong-category spaces as fuel stations that keep your turn alive.

walkthrough.title: Face Your Weakest Category Early
walkthrough.text: It feels natural to avoid your weakest category, but this is a trap. You need all six wedges to win, and your weakest category will not get easier over time. Attempt it early when you have many turns to recover from a wrong answer. Getting your hardest wedge out of the way early relieves pressure and lets you focus on efficient routing for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Strengths for the Final Question
walkthrough.text: When you reach the center hub with all six wedges, your opponents choose the category for your final question. They will pick whatever they think is your weakest subject. Be mentally prepared for this and do not show which categories make you nervous during the game. If opponents cannot identify your weakness, they might guess wrong and give you an easier final question.

walkthrough.title: Team Strategy: Pair Complementary Knowledge
walkthrough.text: In team play, form teams that cover each other's weak categories. If one player knows Sports and Science but struggles with Entertainment and History, pair them with someone who has the opposite strengths. During questions, let the team member with the most confidence in that category take the lead. A balanced team rarely has a category they cannot handle.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest Trivial Pursuit mistakes all in one place. First, moving randomly without targeting a headquarters. Second, avoiding your weakest category until it is too late. Third, heading to the center hub without all six wedges. Fourth, forgetting that you can move in any direction, including backward along the ring or down spokes. Fifth, spending too long on any single question when there is no official time limit and other players are waiting.

walkthrough.title: Keep the Game Moving
walkthrough.text: Trivial Pursuit is a social game and long pauses between turns can slow the fun. Consider using a house rule timer of sixty seconds per question to keep things lively. Read questions clearly and at a good pace. If a player takes a long streak of correct answers, enjoy the excitement rather than getting frustrated. The game is more fun when everyone stays engaged between turns by listening to the questions and mentally answering along.

bullets: Identify target HQ before rolling — count spaces, choose direction
Route through multiple headquarters in sequence

bullets: Spokes = express lanes through the center hub

bullets: Random movement without a headquarters target
Avoiding weakest category until endgame

summary.title: Keep the Game Moving
bullets: Consider a 60-second house rule timer per question
Stay engaged between turns — mentally answer along

walkthrough.title: Optimal Hub-Spoke Routing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players map out multi-turn routes before the game begins. The ideal path starts from the hub, collects two or three wedges on one side of the board, dives back through the hub via a spoke, then collects the remaining wedges on the other side. This cross-board route minimizes wasted moves. Count the spaces between headquarters and figure out which die rolls land you exactly where you need to be.

walkthrough.title: Streaking Through Strong Categories
walkthrough.text: The streak mechanic, where correct answers give you extra rolls, is the most powerful feature in the game. Plan your route so that spaces between headquarters are your strongest categories. Each correct answer on the way to a headquarters gives you a free roll. A streak of three or four correct answers can carry you from one headquarters to the next in a single turn, effectively doubling your movement speed.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to which categories your opponents answer correctly and incorrectly throughout the game. This information becomes critical at the endgame when you choose the category for their final question. Track their weak spots mentally. If a player consistently fails History questions, remember that for when they reach the center hub. Choosing the right final question category can deny a victory.

walkthrough.title: Disguising Your Weak Category
walkthrough.text: Since opponents choose your final question category, it pays to disguise your weakness. If you know History is your worst subject, do not groan every time you land on a yellow space. Show confidence regardless of the category. Some advanced players even make false complaints about categories they are actually strong in, hoping opponents will choose that category for the final question instead of the real weakness.

walkthrough.title: The Final Approach
walkthrough.text: When approaching the center hub for the final question, count the spaces carefully. You need an exact roll to land on the hub. If you are five spaces away, you need exactly a five. Position yourself so that multiple die rolls could land you on the hub. Being two or three spaces away gives you the best odds because more die outcomes will work. Avoid being exactly one space away since only rolling a one gets you there.

walkthrough.title: Psychological Pressure and Pacing
walkthrough.text: When a player reaches the hub for their final question, the pressure is intense. As the reader, read the question clearly and at a steady pace. As a fellow competitor choosing the category, pick it quickly and confidently. If you are the one answering, take a breath before responding. Many final-question failures come from rushing an answer under pressure. A calm, deliberate response wins more games than a panicked guess.

walkthrough.title: House Rules That Improve the Game
walkthrough.text: Several popular house rules can make Trivial Pursuit more enjoyable. A sixty-second timer keeps the game moving. Allowing partial credit for close answers reduces frustration. Requiring a two-thirds majority vote on whether an answer is close enough prevents arguments. For shorter games, require only four of six wedges. These modifications keep the game fresh and reduce the common complaint that Trivial Pursuit can drag on too long.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Hub Control
walkthrough.text: In competitive games, multiple players may approach six wedges at the same time. The player who reaches the hub first has an advantage, but failing the final question means leaving and returning. If you see an opponent heading for the hub, consider whether you should rush to beat them there or slow down and ensure you are better prepared. Sometimes letting an opponent fail their final question and then swooping in on your next turn is the winning move.

summary.title: Optimal Hub-Spoke Routing
bullets: Map multi-turn routes before the game starts
Collect wedges on one side → hub → collect on the other side

summary.title: Streaking Through Strong Categories
bullets: Route through strong-category spaces between headquarters
3-4 correct answers in a row = HQ to HQ in a single turn

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Track which categories opponents fail throughout the game
Use this info to choose their final question category

summary.title: Disguising Your Weak Category
bullets: Don't show frustration on weak categories
Bluff weakness in strong categories to misdirect opponents

summary.title: The Final Approach
bullets: Position 2-3 spaces from hub for best exact-roll odds
1 space away = only a die roll of 1 works

summary.title: Psychological Pressure and Pacing
bullets: Take a breath before answering final question
Calm, deliberate answers > panicked guesses

summary.title: House Rules That Improve the Game
bullets: 60-second timer, partial credit, 4-of-6 wedges for shorter games
2/3 majority vote on close answers prevents arguments

summary.title: Endgame Hub Control
bullets: Rushing to hub first has risk — failing means leaving and returning
Sometimes letting opponent fail first is the winning play

entries.definition: Questions about countries, capitals, landmarks, oceans, rivers, maps, and world geography.
Questions about movies, TV shows, music, celebrities, video games, and pop culture.
Questions about historical events, wars, famous figures, dates, and civilizations.
Questions about books, authors, paintings, sculptures, art movements, and literary works.
Questions about biology, chemistry, physics, animals, plants, weather, and the natural world.
Questions about sports, athletes, games, hobbies, recreation, and leisure activities.
Circular pie-shaped token with six empty wedge slots. Players insert colored wedges as they earn them from each category headquarters.
Small colored plastic piece inserted into the playing piece when a player answers correctly at that category's headquarters. Six wedges (one per category) needed to attempt the final question.
Card with six questions (one per category color). Reader selects the question matching the color of the space the active player landed on. Card goes to the back of the box after use.
The central space of the board where all players start and must return to after collecting all six wedges. Must be reached by exact die roll for the final question attempt.
The continuous circular track of colored spaces forming the perimeter of the board. Players move clockwise or counterclockwise along this ring.
One of six straight paths connecting the outer ring to the center hub. Each spoke is a single category color. Used as shortcuts to cross the board quickly.
Six special spaces on the outer ring where each spoke begins. Landing here and answering correctly earns the corresponding colored wedge. The only spaces where wedges can be earned.
Special space on some board editions that grants an immediate re-roll without answering a question. Free extra movement.

The winning question asked when a player with all six wedges reaches the center hub. Opponents choose the category. Correct answer wins the game; incorrect answer means leaving the hub and returning later.
A chain of consecutive correct answers within a single turn. Each correct answer grants another die roll. No limit on streak length. Key momentum mechanic.
Requirement to land on the center hub by rolling the exact number of spaces needed. Cannot overshoot the hub. Being 2-3 spaces away gives the best statistical odds.
Variant where players form teams sharing one playing piece. Team discusses before giving one answer. Works well for large groups. Pair complementary category strengths.
